feed.title: Combined summary - [BIP Draft] Segregated Data: a prunable, script-isolated block region for data carriage

entry.title: Combined summary - [BIP Draft] Segregated Data: a prunable, script-isolated block region for data carriage
entry.summary: The concept of Segregated Data (SegData) provides a structured solution for managing arbitrary data within the Bitcoin network through a soft-fork implementation. This new block region allows for the storage of data that doesn't necessarily involve value transfer, such as application-layer assets, timestamping, and attestations. By creating a separate Merkle root specifically for these entries, they are included in-block but can be pruned by node operators beyond the standard retention window, thereby not burdening all nodes with permanent storage costs. SegData is designed to follow the pattern set by BIP-141, incorporating elements like a base and extended serialization for blocks, an extended weight formula, and witness v2 reference outputs which are value-zero, unspendable, and excluded from the UTXO set. This design aims to ensure that the stored data remains prunable—meaning it can be deleted after it's no longer needed for consensus purposes—and does not impact transaction validation processes due to script isolation, ensuring that no opcode can interact with the data entries. The introduction of SegData seeks to address the limitations observed with OP_RETURN outputs, which currently serve a similar purpose but result in permanent data storage on all nodes and contribute to blockchain bloat. By offering a prunable option at a witness discount, SegData provides a more resource-efficient alternative while maintaining the integrity and functionality required for certain types of non-value data on the blockchain. There are still open questions and considerations surrounding SegData, such as deciding whether to share the witness version and length slot with BIP-360 P2MR or to use a dedicated version, the feasibility of committing per-reference entry length for better transaction weight and fee rate calculation, and the appropriate scope boundary for future restrictions on existing vectors. These aspects require further discussion and consensus within the community. For further details, the draft proposals for SegData and its peer services can be reviewed in their respective Bitcoin Improvement Proposals, found here: [Consensus BIP](https://github.com/MrHash/bips/blob/4eeeb0afbb9d256d264225801e635d2df1cc875f/bip-segdata.md) and [Peer-services BIP](https://github.com/MrHash/bips/blob/4eeeb0afbb9d256d264225801e635d2df1cc875f/bip-segdata-peer-services.md). These documents outline the technical implementations and the rationale behind the proposed changes, aiming to enhance the scalability and efficiency of data management within the Bitcoin network.
